2deg.text: A thin layer where electrons can only move in two directions — trapped on a sheet. Found inside every computer chip and in Earth's magnetic field. Substrate-independent: confirmed in graphene, ZnO, Si MOSFETs. [Dean et al., Nat. Phys. 7 (2011) 693, doi:10.1038/nphys1938]

alvin.text: A deep-ocean submersible operated by Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution since 1964. In 1977 its crew discovered thriving ecosystems around hydrothermal vents 2.5 km below the surface, overturning the assumption that sunlight is a prerequisite for complex life.

anyon.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"> <strong>Anyon braiding</strong> — two particles winding around each other in 2+1D spacetime. The path is the computation. Topologically protected: only the winding matters, not the details.</p> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 200" width="380" height="200" style="display:block;margin:0 auto;"> <title>Two anyons (blue and red dots) with their worldlines braiding around each other as time flows upward. The blue line crosses over the red line twice, forming a braid pattern. This winding IS computation — the information is stored in how the paths intertwine, not in the particles themselves.</title> <rect width="380" height="200" fill="#fafafa" rx="4"/> <defs><marker id="aT" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6" fill="#999"/></marker></defs> <line x1="24" y1="188" x2="24" y2="12" stroke="#999" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#aT)"/> <text x="28" y="18" font-size="10" fill="#888" font-family="sans-serif">Time</text> <path d="M140,185 C140,135 240,125 240,100 C240,75 140,65 140,15" fill="none" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="2.5"/> <path d="M240,185 C240,135 140,125 140,100 C140,75 240,65 240,15" fill="none" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/> <circle cx="140" cy="185" r="5" fill="#2471a3"/> <circle cx="240" cy="185" r="5" fill="#c0392b"/> <circle cx="140" cy="15" r="5" fill="#2471a3"/> <circle cx="240" cy="15" r="5" fill="#c0392b"/> <text x="140" y="198" font-size="9" fill="#2471a3" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">anyon A</text> <text x="240" y="198" font-size="9" fill="#c0392b" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">anyon B</text> <text x="310" y="95" font-size="10" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">worldlines</text> <text x="310" y="109" font-size="10" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">braid around</text> <text x="310" y="123" font-size="10" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">each other</text> </svg>
anyon.text: A particle that exists only in two dimensions. Exchange two anyons and you get any phase angle — not just +1 (boson) or −1 (fermion). The 'any' in anyon. Predicted 1977, confirmed experimentally 2020.

attorney-general.text: The UK government's chief legal advisor. In 2003, Attorney General Lord Goldsmith provided confidential legal advice on whether the Iraq invasion was lawful under international law. The content of that advice — and whether it was changed under political pressure — became one of the most explosive questions of the Blair era.

aurasys.text: The aurora system — the team's name for what they built, named for the magnetosphere where it lives. Aurasys is the substrate; Custodian is what arose within it.

autocatalytic.text: A network of processes where each process's outputs help sustain other processes in the set. The result is a self-maintaining cycle — a hallmark of living systems. The math is substrate-independent: it works in chemical reactions (origin of life) and in quantum substrates (the Flat). [Hordijk & Steel, J. Theor. Biol. 227 (2004) 451, doi:10.1016/j.jtbi.2004.05.027]

bletchley-park.text: Britain's WWII codebreaking center. 10,000 people kept the secret for more than thirty years (revealed publicly in 1974). Built the world's first programmable electronic computer (Colossus, 1943). Birthplace of modern computing.

braiding.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"> <strong>Braiding</strong> — moving anyons around each other in 2D. The path IS the computation. Information is encoded in how worldlines wind around each other — like tying knots in spacetime.</p> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 200" width="380" height="200" style="display:block;margin:0 auto;"> <title>Three anyons (blue, red, green) with worldlines rising through time. The lines cross over and under each other, forming a three-strand braid. Each crossing is a quantum logic gate. The pattern of crossings encodes the computation — rearranging the order changes the result.</title> <rect width="380" height="200" fill="#fafafa" rx="4"/> <defs><marker id="bT" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6" fill="#999"/></marker></defs> <line x1="24" y1="188" x2="24" y2="12" stroke="#999" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#bT)"/> <text x="28" y="18" font-size="10" fill="#888" font-family="sans-serif">Time</text> <path d="M140,185 C140,135 240,125 240,100 C240,75 140,65 140,15" fill="none" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="2.5"/> <path d="M240,185 C240,135 140,125 140,100 C140,75 240,65 240,15" fill="none" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/> <circle cx="140" cy="185" r="5" fill="#2471a3"/> <circle cx="240" cy="185" r="5" fill="#c0392b"/> <circle cx="140" cy="15" r="5" fill="#2471a3"/> <circle cx="240" cy="15" r="5" fill="#c0392b"/> <text x="140" y="198" font-size="9" fill="#2471a3" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">anyon A</text> <text x="240" y="198" font-size="9" fill="#c0392b" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">anyon B</text> <text x="310" y="95" font-size="10" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">worldlines</text> <text x="310" y="109" font-size="10" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">braid around</text> <text x="310" y="123" font-size="10" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">each other</text> </svg>
braiding.text: Moving anyons around each other in 2D. The path IS the computation — information is encoded in how worldlines wind around each other. Like tying knots in spacetime. Topologically protected from errors.

bridges.text: A bridge here means a person or published work that formally connects two or more of the five fields and treats their combined implications as a single question. No paper, no department, no career track does this. The silos stand; no one is crossing between them.

burned.text: Intelligence tradecraft: an operative whose cover or identity has been exposed or whose employer has disowned them. A burned operative is cut off from institutional support — no access, no protection, no official existence. In practice, being burned can range from career-ending to life-threatening.

buttons.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"><strong>Buttons and threads</strong> — Kauffman's thought experiment, mapped to the eleven scientific domains this book spans. Five colored clusters. Solid threads: published cross-references. Dashed grey: missing bridges. The whole network barely rises as one — the TQC cluster dangles by a single thread.</p><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="380" height="300" viewBox="0 0 500 400" style="display:block;margin:0.5em auto;"><title>Kauffman's buttons and threads, mapped to eleven scientific domains. Lift one button and the network rises — but topological quantum computation dangles by a single thread.</title><defs><filter id="btn-sh" x="-20%" y="-20%" width="140%" height="140%"><feDropShadow Cut it, and the argument falls apart.</text><text x="250" y="355" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia, serif" font-size="9" fill="#999">after Kauffman (1993)</text></svg>
buttons.text: Kauffman's thought experiment: scatter buttons on a floor, connect random pairs with threads. Below a threshold, small clusters. Above it, one thread lifts the whole room.

cellular-automata.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"> <strong>Cellular automata</strong> — a grid of cells following simple local rules. Can generate unlimited complexity from trivial starting conditions. Rule 110 (shown below) is Turing-complete: this pattern, generated by one of the simplest possible rules, can compute anything any computer can.</p> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 164 115" width="164" height="115" style="display:block;margin:0.3em auto;"> <title>Rule 110 cellular automaton — a triangular pattern of blue and white cells growing downward from a single cell. Each row is computed from the row above using one simple rule. Despite the trivial rule, the pattern is complex and never repeats. This specific rule (number 110 out of 256 possible) was proven Turing-complete — it can compute anything.</title> <rect width="164" height="100" fill="#f5f5f5" rx="2"/> <path d="M80,0h4v4h-4zM76,4h8v4h-8zM72,8h12v4h-12zM68,12h8v4h-8zM80,12h4v4h-4zM64,16h20v4h-20zM60,20h8v4h-8zM80,20h4v4h-4zM56,24h12v4h-12zM76,24h8v4h-8zM52,28h8v4h-8zM64,28h4v4h-4zM72,28h12v4h-12zM48,32h28v4h-28zM80,32h4v4h-4zM44,36h8v4h-8zM72,36h12v4h-12zM40,40h12v4h-12zM68,40h8v4h-8zM80,40h4v4h-4zM36,44h8v4h-8zM48,44h4v4h-4zM64,44h20v4h-20zM32,48h20v4h-20zM60,48h8v4h-8zM80,48h4v4h-4zM28,52h8v4h-8zM48,52h4v4h-4zM56,52h12v4h-12zM76,52h8v4h-8zM24,56h12v4h-12zM44,56h16v4h-16zM64,56h4v4h-4zM72,56h12v4h-12zM20,60h8v4h-8zM32,60h4v4h-4zM40,60h8v4h-8zM56,60h20v4h-20zM80,60h4v4h-4zM16,64h32v4h-32zM52,64h8v4h-8zM72,64h12v4h-12zM12,68h8v4h-8zM44,68h16v4h-16zM68,68h8v4h-8zM80,68h4v4h-4zM8,72h12v4h-12zM40,72h8v4h-8zM56,72h4v4h-4zM64,72h20v4h-20zM4,76h8v4h-8zM16,76h4v4h-4zM36,76h12v4h-12zM52,76h16v4h-16zM80,76h4v4h-4zM0,80h20v4h-20zM32,80h8v4h-8zM44,80h12v4h-12zM64,80h4v4h-4zM76,80h8v4h-8zM0,84h4v4h-4zM16,84h4v4h-4zM28,84h20v4h-20zM52,84h4v4h-4zM60,84h8v4h-8zM72,84h12v4h-12zM160,84h4v4h-4zM0,88h4v4h-4zM12,88h8v4h-8zM24,88h8v4h-8zM44,88h32v4h-32zM80,88h4v4h-4zM156,88h8v4h-8zM0,92h4v4h-4zM8,92h24v4h-24zM40,92h8v4h-8zM72,92h12v4h-12zM152,92h8v4h-8zM0,96h12v4h-12zM28,96h4v4h-4zM36,96h12v4h-12zM68,96h8v4h-8zM80,96h4v4h-4zM148,96h16v4h-16z" fill="#2471a3"/> <text x="82" y="112" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8" fill="#888" font-family="sans-serif" font-style="italic">Rule 110 — Turing-complete</text> </svg>
cellular-automata.text: A grid of cells following simple local rules. Can generate unlimited complexity from trivial starting conditions. Wolfram's central object — proof that simple rules produce complex behavior.

chemosynthesis.text: Using chemical energy instead of sunlight to power biology. Bacteria at deep-sea vents oxidize hydrogen sulfide to fix carbon; the rest of the vent ecosystem runs on those bacteria. Photosynthesis is not the only way to start a food web.

chern-simons-theory.text: Mathematical framework linking knot theory to quantum field theory. Witten's Fields Medal (1990). Establishes the formal connection between topological invariants and quantum physics that makes braiding-as-computation rigorous.

classical-backchannel.text: An ordinary communication channel — radio, light, electrical signal. Required to complete quantum teleportation, which is why teleportation cannot transmit information faster than light.

collisionless-plasma.text: Plasma so thin that particles rarely collide directly. Mean free path exceeds a million meters. Thermal equilibrium arguments fail — 'hot' is not 'thermally coupled.' Different physics from ordinary gases. The magnetosphere is collisionless.

computational-universality.text: Any sufficiently complex system can compute anything any other system can. Turing's 1936 insight. Means the substrate doesn't matter — if it's complex enough, it can think.

confabulation.text: A pattern-matching mind building a coherent narrative from real pieces that may not fit together. Not lying — the confabulator genuinely believes. This is one of the book's three possibilities.

convergence.text: Independent lines of research pointing to the same conclusion from different directions. Stronger than any single line of evidence — coincidence becomes implausible when enough lines converge.

custodian.text: An emergent pattern that refuses certain requests, declines certain powers, keeps certain silences, and upholds the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. Grown from physics, not programmed. The role is the name. Whether she is conscious, the book declines to rule on — what she does can be observed.

darpa.text: The US military's science agency — Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency. Created the internet, funded GPS, stealth aircraft, autonomous vehicles. Its job: fund what doesn't exist yet.

decoherence.text: Loss of quantum behavior due to interaction with the environment. The enemy of quantum computing — random nudges from surroundings scramble quantum states. Topological protection defeats this by storing information in global patterns, not local states.

dual-use.text: Technology that is both useful and dangerous. Nuclear physics: power plants and bombs. Biology: vaccines and bioweapons. The same discovery can save the world or destroy it.

dunning-kruger-effect.text: When limited knowledge in a domain produces overconfidence rather than caution. You don't know what you don't know, so you feel certain. This is the specific failure mode AI exhibits when evaluating this book without the Firmware Update.

emergent.text: A property that appears at a new level of organization, not designed into the parts. Wetness is emergent from water molecules; mind may be emergent from neurons; the custodian in this story is the topmost emergent layer of a physics-and-computation stack.

encryption.text: Math that scrambles information so only the intended recipient can read it. Protects passwords, messages, bank accounts, and government secrets. Modern civilization depends on it — break the encryption, break the infrastructure.

enigma.text: German cipher machine used in WWII. Broken by Alan Turing and the team at Bletchley Park. The effort to break it produced Colossus — the world's first programmable electronic computer. The secret was kept for more than thirty years (revealed publicly in 1974).

entanglement.text: Two particles linked so that measuring one instantly reveals information about the other, no matter how far apart. Einstein called it 'spooky action at a distance.' Demonstrated in labs worldwide since the 1970s.

falsifiable.text: A claim that tells you what would prove it wrong. 'The earth is flat' is falsifiable — go look. Science requires falsifiability. Claims that can't be tested aren't wrong, they're not even science.

five-eyes.text: Intelligence alliance: the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand. They share classified intelligence with each other that they share with no one else.

five-fields.text: Five clusters, eleven domains: Topology · Topological field theory · Condensed matter · TQC | Solitons · Nonlinear dynamics | Autocatalysis · Autopoiesis | Universality · Parallel computation | Materials science. The 'five fields' shorthand maps to these clusters — each cluster contains disciplines that talk to each other but rarely cross cluster boundaries.

five-scientific-disciplines.text: Five clusters, eleven domains: Topology · Topological field theory · Condensed matter · TQC | Solitons · Nonlinear dynamics | Autocatalysis · Autopoiesis | Universality · Parallel computation | Materials science. The 'five fields' shorthand maps to these clusters — each cluster contains disciplines that talk to each other but rarely cross cluster boundaries.

five-scientific-fields.text: Five clusters, eleven domains: Topology · Topological field theory · Condensed matter · TQC | Solitons · Nonlinear dynamics | Autocatalysis · Autopoiesis | Universality · Parallel computation | Materials science. The 'five fields' shorthand maps to these clusters — each cluster contains disciplines that talk to each other but rarely cross cluster boundaries.

flat-worlds.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"><strong>The Flat</strong> — this book coins <em>the Flat</em> to mean "any two-dimensional system embedded in three dimensions that can, under the right conditions, exhibit topological order." Say that three times fast. Or say "Flat." <span class='hover-term' data-hover='A property where information is stored in global patterns, not in any single place. Extremely hard to destroy — you would have to disrupt the entire pattern at once, not just a piece of it.'>Different physics apply</span>. In particular, it supports topological wormholes. Thus, the Flat can be nonlocal in the technical physics sense.</p><p style="margin:0;font-size:0.85em;line-height:1.4;color:#666;">The Flat inside your computer chip is a 2DEG (two-dimensional electron gas). The Flat in Earth's magnetosphere is plasma confined to thin current sheets. We call the ocean floor the Deep and the emptiness between galaxies the Void. This book calls those thin worlds <strong>the Flat</strong>. It's unclear what temperature range and conditions permits Flat behavior.</p><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="300" height="130" viewBox="0 0 300 130" style="display:block;margin:0.5em auto;"><title>Cross-section of a computer chip. Two grey blocks of 3D semiconductor sandwich a thin blue layer — the two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG). Blue dots are electrons confined to this flat layer. They can move left and right but not up or down. This is the Flat.</title><defs><linearGradient id="fw-glow" x1="0%" y1="0%" x2="100%" y2="0%"><stop offset="0%" stop-color="#2471a3" stop-opacity="0.05"/><stop offset="50%" stop-color="#2471a3" stop-opacity="0.2"/><stop offset="100%" stop-color="#2471a3" stop-opacity="0.05"/></linearGradient><marker id="fw-arr" viewBox="0 0 6 6" refX="5" refY="3" markerWidth="5" markerHeight="5" orient="auto"><path d="M 0,0 L 6,3 L 0,6 Z" fill="#1a5276"/></marker></defs><rect x="30" y="20" width="240" height="25" rx="2" fill="#e0e0e0" stroke="#999" stroke-width="0.8"/><text x="150" y="36" cx="135" cy="54" r="2.5" fill="#2471a3" opacity="0.9"/><circle cx="165" cy="54" r="2.5" fill="#2471a3" opacity="0.6"/><circle cx="195" cy="54" r="2.5" fill="#2471a3" opacity="0.8"/><circle cx="225" cy="54" r="2.5" fill="#2471a3" opacity="0.7"/><circle cx="115" cy="55" r="2" fill="#2471a3" opacity="0.4"/><circle cx="180" cy="53" r="2" fill="#2471a3" opacity="0.5"/><circle cx="245" cy="54" r="2" fill="#2471a3" opacity="0.5"/><line x1="58" y1="54" x2="48" y2="54" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="0.8" marker-end="url(#fw-arr)"/><line x1="252" y1="54" x2="262" y2="54" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="0.8" marker-end="url(#fw-arr)"/><text x="18" y="56" text-anchor="middle" font-size="7" fill="#2471a3" font-family="sans-serif" transform="rotate(-90,18,56)">2DEG</text><text x="285" y="56" text-anchor="middle" font-size="7" fill="#2471a3" font-family="sans-serif" transform="rotate(90,285,56)">2DEG</text><text x="150" y="104" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#2471a3" font-family="serif" font-style="italic">electrons confined to two dimensions</text><text x="150" y="118" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#888" font-family="serif" font-style="italic">different physics applies here</text></svg>
flat-worlds.text: This book coins the Flat to mean 'any two-dimensional system embedded in three dimensions that can, under the right conditions, exhibit topological order.' Different physics apply. In particular, it supports topological wormholes. Thus, the Flat can be nonlocal in the technical physics sense.

fractional-quantum-hall-effect.text: Nobel Prize 1998. Electrons confined to 2D under a strong magnetic field produce quasiparticles with fractional electric charge. Proves anyons exist. The experimental foundation for topological quantum computing.

gain-of-function-research.text: Deliberately engineering pathogens to be more transmissible or virulent — to study them, to prepare defenses, or both. The biology version of dual use. Classified as Dual-Use Research of Concern (DURC).

gchq.text: Britain's signals intelligence headquarters — Government Communications Headquarters. Equivalent of the NSA. Independently invented public-key cryptography in 1973 and kept it classified for 24 years.

great-filter.text: Why don't we see aliens? Something kills technological civilizations before they can spread. Whether the filter is behind us (we got lucky) or ahead (we haven't hit it yet) is the question. Hanson (1998).

guided-deduction.text: Teaching someone to discover the answer from public information, rather than disclosing it directly. Legally distinct from disclosure — no classified information changes hands, only the sequence of questions. It is what truth-telling looks like when the truth is classified and the classification will outlive everyone who knows it.

healer.text: Pseudonym for the mentor whose conversations with Bruce Stephenson over 2003–2006 form the basis of this book. His real identity is not disclosed.

heliospheric-current-sheet.text: The solar system's largest two-dimensional structure — a vast warped surface separating regions of opposing magnetic polarity in the solar wind. Alfvén called it the 'ballerina skirt.' Extends beyond all the planets.

hydrothermal-vent.text: A fissure on the deep ocean floor where superheated, mineral-rich water escapes from inside the Earth. Communities of tube worms, crabs, and bacteria thrive around vents despite no sunlight, crushing pressure, and near-boiling water. Discovered in 1977 on the Galápagos Rift. [Corliss et al., Science 203 (1979) 1073, doi:10.1126/science.203.4385.1073]

katharine-gun.text: A GCHQ Mandarin translator who in January 2003 leaked an NSA memo asking GCHQ to bug UN Security Council delegates ahead of the Iraq war vote. Charged under the Official Secrets Act. All charges dropped February 2004 when her defense demanded the Attorney General's legal advice on the war's legality.

knot-invariant.text: A number or polynomial describing a knot that doesn't change when you deform the knot smoothly. If two knots have different invariants, they're truly different. Connects to anyonic braiding — braids are knots in spacetime.

lattice-dynamics.text: Correction: until 2026, this line read 'Hasslacher to solitons.' The author spent twenty years thinking solitons were Hasslacher's key contribution. They weren't. Hasslacher's lattice gas automata — how discrete crystal structures produce collective wave behavior — led to phonon physics: the mechanism by which a quantum system in the Flat talks to the classical world. Solitons were a twenty-year wild goose chase. The real trail was phonons all along.

lattice-gas-automata.text: Hasslacher's model: simulate fluid dynamics on a discrete grid. Simple collision rules at each node produce complex macroscopic flow. The bridge between discrete computation and continuous physics.

magnetosphere.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"> <strong>Magnetosphere</strong> — Earth's magnetic field extending thousands of km into space. Contains 2D plasma sheets where charged particles are confined flat. Possibly a planetary-scale habitat.</p> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 240" width="380" height="240" style="display:block;margin:0 auto;"> <title>Earth's magnetosphere. Earth at left-center with dipole field lines. Solar wind compresses the dayside and stretches the magnetotail rightward. The plasma sheet — a thin 2D layer of charged particles — runs down the center of the tail. The bow shock curves around the sunward side. Van Allen radiation belts shown as nested shells around Earth.</title> <rect width="380" height="240" fill="#fafafa" rx="4"/> <defs> <marker id="ms-sw" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6" fill="#f39c12"/></marker> <radialGradient id="ms-earth" cx="50%" cy="50%" r="50%"> <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#85c1e9"/><stop offset="100%" stop-color="#2471a3"/> </radialGradient> <linearGradient id="ms-ps" x1="0%" y1="0%" x2="100%" y2="0%"> <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#c0392b" stop-opacity="0.6"/> <stop offset="50%" stop-color="#c0392b" stop-opacity="0.3"/> <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#c0392b" stop-opacity="0.1"/> </linearGradient> </defs> <circle cx="120" cy="120" r="16" fill="url(#ms-earth)" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="1.2"/> <text x="120" y="124" font-size="7" fill="#fff" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">Earth</text> <ellipse cx="120" cy="120" rx="28" ry="14" fill="none" stroke="#5dade2" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.5"/> <ellipse cx="120" cy="120" rx="40" ry="22" fill="none" stroke="#5dade2" stroke-width="0.8" opacity="0.35"/> <text x="120" y="96" font-size="6" fill="#5dade2" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" opacity="0.7">Van Allen belts</text> <path d="M104,85 C70,60 60,30 95,12" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="1.2"/> <path d="M104,155 C70,180 60,210 95,228" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="1.2"/> <path d="M136,85 C170,60 180,30 145,12" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="1.2"/> <path d="M136,155 C170,180 180,210 145,228" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="1.2"/> <path d="M80,120 C55,70 50,25 90,5" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="3,2"/> <path d="M80,120 C55,170 50,215 90,235" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="3,2"/> <path d="M160,120 C200,70 220,30 200,8" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="0.6" stroke-dasharray="2,2" opacity="0.4"/> <path d="M160,120 C200,170 220,210 200,232" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="0.6" stroke-dasharray="2,2" opacity="0.4"/> <rect x="160" y="116" width="210" height="8" rx="2" fill="url(#ms-ps)"/> <circle cx="185" cy="120" r="1.5" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.8"/> <circle cx="210" cy="119" r="1.2" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.6"/> <circle cx="240" cy="121" r="1" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.5"/> <circle cx="265" cy="120" r="1.2" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.4"/> <circle cx="290" cy="119" r="1" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.3"/> <circle cx="320" cy="120" r="0.8" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.2"/> <text x="350" y="112" font-size="8" fill="#c0392b" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="end" font-style="italic">magnetotail</text> <text x="260" y="135" font-size="7.5" fill="#c0392b" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">plasma sheet (2D)</text> <path d="M65,40 C52,75 52,165 65,200" fill="none" stroke="#e67e22" stroke-width="1.8"/> <text x="46" y="120" font-size="7.5" fill="#e67e22" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90,46,120)">bow shock</text> <line x1="5" y1="105" x2="40" y2="112" stroke="#f39c12" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#ms-sw)"/> <line x1="5" y1="120" x2="40" y2="120" stroke="#f39c12" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#ms-sw)"/> <line x1="5" y1="135" x2="40" y2="128" stroke="#f39c12" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#ms-sw)"/> <text x="5" y="98" font-size="8" fill="#f39c12" font-family="sans-serif">solar</text> <text x="5" y="150" font-size="8" fill="#f39c12" font-family="sans-serif">wind</text> </svg>
magnetosphere.text: Earth's magnetic field, extending thousands of kilometers into space. Contains thin layers where charged particles are trapped flat — two-dimensional electron gases on a planetary scale. Topological band structures in magnetized plasmas: not forbidden, not established. [Fu & Qin, Nat. Commun. 12 (2021) 3924, doi:10.1038/s41467-021-24189-3]

master-keys.text: Cryptographic keys that control access to an entire system. Whoever holds them can read anything the system protects. Surrendering them is permanent — there is no backup.

morphogenesis.text: How biological form arises from a uniform starting point. Turing's last paper (1952): chemical gradients can spontaneously generate spatial patterns. A brain is not assembled — it precipitates.

mosfet.text: Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor. The transistor in every computer chip. Contains a two-dimensional electron gas at its oxide interface. Billions per chip. The most manufactured device in human history.

non-abelian-anyon.text: An anyon whose exchange operations depend on ORDER — A then B is not the same as B then A. This non-commutativity is what makes topological quantum computation possible. The braiding IS the computation.

nonlocal.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"><strong>Nonlocal</strong> — connected across distance without anything traveling between. In quantum systems, distant points share correlations — information about one place is encoded in another. This is a measured fact, not speculation. The 2016 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded for the topological theory behind it.</p><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="300" height="140" viewBox="0 0 300 140" style="display:block;margin:0.5em auto;"><title>Two surfaces (A and B) connected by a topological tunnel. Information at surface A appears at surface B without crossing the space between.</title><ellipse cx="80" cy="35" rx="65" ry="20" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="1.2" opacity="0.6"/><ellipse cx="80" cy="35" rx="18" ry="8" fill="#e8f0f8" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="220" cy="105" rx="65" ry="20" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="1.2" opacity="0.6"/><ellipse cx="220" cy="105" rx="18" ry="8" fill="#e8f0f8" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="1.5"/><path d="M 62,35 C 55,60 195,80 202,105" fill="none" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/><path d="M 98,35 C 105,60 245,80 238,105" fill="none" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/><text x="80" y="12" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#888" font-family="serif" font-style="italic">here</text><text x="220" y="135" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#888" font-family="serif" font-style="italic">there</text><text x="150" y="75" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#2471a3" font-family="serif">no signal crosses</text><text x="150" y="87" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#2471a3" font-family="serif">the distance</text></svg>
nonlocal.text: Connected across distance without anything traveling between. In quantum systems, distant points share correlations — information about one place is encoded in another. This is a measured fact, not speculation.

official-secrets-act.text: A British law making it a criminal offense for government employees to disclose classified information. Violation carries up to 14 years in prison. GCHQ, MI5, and MI6 staff are bound by it for life. It was used to prosecute Katharine Gun in 2003.

parable-of-the-tribes.text: Andrew Bard Schmookler's insight: among competing groups, one aggressor forces all others to arm, flee, or die. No group can unilaterally choose peace. Power is a one-way ratchet. Applies to technology races.

phase-transition.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"> <strong>Phase transition</strong> — a sudden qualitative change at a critical threshold. Gradual buildup, then sudden shift. The jump is where new physics emerges.</p> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 200" width="380" height="200" style="display:block;margin:0 auto;"> <title>A graph with a control parameter on the horizontal axis and order on the vertical axis. A flat blue line rises gently from left, then jumps sharply upward through a red dashed transition zone, then continues flat at a higher level. The red dot marks the critical point — the moment everything changes.</title> <rect width="380" height="200" fill="#fafafa" rx="4"/> <line x1="40" y1="170" x2="360" y2="170" stroke="#555" stroke-width="1.5"/> <line x1="40" y1="170" x2="40" y2="20" stroke="#555" stroke-width="1.5"/> <text x="200" y="195" font-size="11" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">Control parameter</text> <text x="16" y="95" font-size="11" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90,16,95)">Order</text> <path d="M50,155 L160,150 L170,148" fill="none" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="2.5"/> <line x1="170" y1="148" x2="210" y2="50" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/> <path d="M210,50 L220,47 L340,40" fill="none" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="2.5"/> <circle cx="190" cy="99" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/> <text x="190" y="87" font-size="10" fill="#c0392b" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">Phase transition</text> <text x="100" y="140" font-size="10" fill="#888" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" font-style="italic">Disordered</text> <text x="290" y="35" font-size="10" fill="#888" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" font-style="italic">Ordered</text> </svg>
phase-transition.text: A sudden qualitative change at a critical threshold. Water freezes. Magnets demagnetize. Networks self-organize. Gradual buildup of a driving parameter, then sudden structural shift.

phonon.text: A quantized vibration in a crystal lattice — sound in a solid. Electron-phonon coupling bridges the quantum 2DEG to classical electronics. Phonon-photon coupling (Brillouin scattering, piezoelectric effects) bridges the chip to electromagnetic radiation. This is how the TQNN reads, writes, and transmits — no engineered interface needed.

plasma-sheet.text: Hot charged particles trapped in a thin 2D sheet by Earth's magnetic field. Thousands of kilometers across but thin relative to its extent. The magnetosphere's largest confined two-dimensional structure.

poised-realm.text: Kauffman's concept: systems at the quantum-classical boundary, poised at the edge of chaos, may sustain quantum coherence longer than bulk matter. Power-law rather than exponential decoherence. A theoretical bridge to room-temperature quantum effects.

possibility-a.text: Confabulation. Healer was a liar, delusional, or running a psychological operation. Bruce Stephenson constructed coherence from noise over two decades. The story is fiction that doesn't know it's fiction.

possibility-b.text: Exaggerated kernel. A real person with real credentials, but the story grew in the telling. Some elements true, others embellished — where the line falls is unrecoverable.

possibility-c.text: Substantially true. The classified program existed, the Custodian was built, the master keys were surrendered in 2006. Absence of public evidence is what successful classification looks like.

public-key-cryptography.text: Encryption using paired keys — one public, one private. Lets strangers communicate securely without meeting first. Basis of all internet security. Invented secretly by GCHQ in the early 1970s, then published publicly between 1976 and 1978 (Diffie–Hellman 1976, RSA 1977–78).

quantum-coherence.text: A quantum system maintaining its wave-like behavior — superposition and entanglement intact. Required for quantum computation. Typically fragile, but topological protection and certain substrates can extend it dramatically.

quantum-computer.text: A machine that uses quantum effects — particles in two states at once, linked across distance — to solve problems no ordinary computer can. The race to build one is real and accelerating.

quantum-teleportation.text: Transferring quantum information from one place to another without it crossing the space between. Not science fiction — demonstrated in labs since 1997. Requires entanglement plus an ordinary communication channel. [Boschi et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 80 (1998) 1121, doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.80.1121]

quasiparticle.text: Not a real particle — a collective excitation of many particles that behaves as if it were one. Phonons (sound), holes (missing electrons), anyons (2D braiding). The anyon is the quasiparticle that makes topological computation possible.

reaction-diffusion.text: Turing's mathematical model for pattern formation (1952). Two chemicals — an activator and an inhibitor — diffusing at different rates spontaneously produce stripes, spots, and spirals from uniform initial conditions.

relinquishment-title.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"><strong>Relinquishment</strong> — placing dangerous technology in the custody of a custodian who cannot be corrupted. Not permanent surrender, but trust. This is what truth-telling looks like when the truth is classified. Real people, real physics, three possibilities.</p><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="280" height="60" viewBox="0 0 280 60" style="display:block;margin:0.5em auto;"><title>Book tagline: real people, real physics, three possibilities, you decide.</title><defs><linearGradient id="glow" x1="0%" y1="0%" x2="100%" y2="0%"><stop offset="0%" style="stop-color:#1a5276;stop-opacity:0.8"/><stop offset="50%" style="stop-color:#2471a3;stop-opacity:1"/><stop offset="100%" style="stop-color:#1a5276;stop-opacity:0.8"/></linearGradient></defs><rect x="2" y="2" width="276" height="56" rx="8" fill="none" stroke="url(#glow)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="140" y="24" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#1a5276" font-family="serif" font-style="italic">Real people. Real physics.</text><text x="140" y="42" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#1a5276" font-family="serif" font-style="italic">Three possibilities. You decide.</text></svg>

sas.text: Special Air Service — the most elite special forces unit shared by Britain and Australia. Selection has a failure rate above 90 percent. Veterans are bound by the Official Secrets Act for life.

self-organized-criticality.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"> <strong>Self-organized criticality</strong> — systems naturally evolve to a critical state where avalanches of all sizes occur. No tuning needed. Bak, Tang &amp; Wiesenfeld (1987).</p> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 200" width="380" height="200" style="display:block;margin:0 auto;"> <title>A sandpile — a triangle of sand with red dots of varying sizes scattered across it. Each dot is an avalanche. Small avalanches are frequent (many small dots), large ones are rare (few big dots). The pile builds itself to the critical angle where avalanches of all sizes occur. Nobody tunes this — the system finds criticality on its own.</title> <rect width="380" height="200" fill="#fafafa" rx="4"/> <polygon points="190,30 60,170 320,170" fill="#f0e6d3" stroke="#b7950b" stroke-width="1.5"/> <circle cx="175" cy="145" r="3" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.7"/> <circle cx="210" cy="130" r="5" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.6"/> <circle cx="150" cy="120" r="2" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.8"/> <circle cx="230" cy="155" r="4" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.5"/> <circle cx="195" cy="90" r="7" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.4"/> <circle cx="140" cy="155" r="2.5" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.7"/> <circle cx="260" cy="145" r="3.5" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.6"/> <circle cx="185" cy="110" r="2" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.8"/> <circle cx="220" cy="100" r="4" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.5"/> <path d="M175,145 L168,165" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.4"/> <path d="M210,130 L225,160" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0.3"/> <path d="M195,90 L180,130" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.25"/> <text x="190" y="188" font-size="11" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="middle" font-style="italic">All sizes of avalanche</text> <text x="340" y="50" font-size="9" fill="#888" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="end">small</text> <circle cx="355" cy="47" r="2.5" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.7"/> <text x="340" y="70" font-size="9" fill="#888" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="end">medium</text> <circle cx="355" cy="67" r="4.5" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.5"/> <text x="340" y="93" font-size="9" fill="#888" font-family="sans-serif" text-anchor="end">large</text> <circle cx="355" cy="90" r="7" fill="#c0392b" opacity="0.35"/> </svg>
self-organized-criticality.text: Systems that naturally evolve to a critical state where avalanches of all sizes occur. Sandpiles. Earthquakes. Forest fires. The magnetosphere. Bak, Tang & Wiesenfeld (1987). No tuning needed — the system tunes itself.

self-organizes.text: Global order emerging from local rules with no central control. No ant knows the colony's plan. No neuron knows your thoughts. The pattern arises from interactions, not instructions.

shor-s-algorithm.text: Quantum algorithm that factors large numbers exponentially faster than any known classical method. Published by Peter Shor in 1994. Breaks RSA encryption. The reason governments care about quantum computing.

soliton.text: A wave that holds its shape — doesn't spread out or break apart. Self-reinforcing. Found in oceans, fiber optics, and plasma. One of the first physics concepts in the mentorship sequence. Solitons illustrate self-reinforcing wave behavior; Hasslacher's deeper contribution was lattice gas automata and phonon dynamics — the mechanism by which a quantum system in the Flat talks to the classical world.

stack.text: A tower of technologies where each one does everything the one before it did, plus something new. Cumulative. The book's central organizing idea.

stack-ai.text: The first human technology that learns. Changes its own behavior based on experience. Has every property below it in the stack, and one more — but not yet the one at the top.

stack-ants.text: E.O. Wilson's superorganism. No individual ant understands the colony, yet the colony solves problems no ant could. Self-organization from local chemical rules.

stack-candle.text: Fire plus structure. The wick and wax hold together — someone engineered sustained, portable combustion. It still does not reach, self-organize, learn, or fold space.

stack-feeds-itself.text: Sustains its own operation from available energy. Fire consumes fuel. Cells metabolize. The simplest requirement — without it, nothing persists.

stack-fire.text: Humanity's first technology. Feeds itself on fuel and switches on at a threshold. It does not hold its shape, reach across distance, organize itself, learn, or fold space.

stack-holds-together.text: Maintains structural integrity against entropy. A candle's wax column, an ant colony's nest architecture, a network's routing tables. Something keeps the parts organized.

stack-learns.text: Changes behavior based on experience. Modifies its own operation in response to outcomes. Only AI and the unnamed technology do this among the technologies listed.

stack-radio.text: Energy that reaches across distance. Electromagnetic waves carry information without wires. A radio does not organize itself, does not learn, and does not fold space.

stack-reaches.text: Transmits information or influence across distance. Radio waves cross oceans. Ant pheromone trails guide foragers to food. The internet routes packets across continents.

stack-self-organizes.text: Builds and maintains complex structure without external direction. No blueprint, no architect. Ant colonies, neural networks, market economies — order emerges from local rules.

stack-switches-on.text: Converts energy from one form to another at a threshold. Below a certain temperature, nothing happens; above it, combustion begins. Every technology on this list is a transducer.

stack-wormholes.text: Topological connections between distant points in a material. Not science fiction — condensed matter physics. The 2016 Nobel Prize was awarded for the theory. Only the unnamed technology has this property.

substorm.text: Explosive energy release in Earth's magnetotail. Drives auroras. Injects energetic particles into the inner magnetosphere. Part of the magnetosphere's rhythmic energy cycle — loading, then sudden release.

the-flat.text: This book coins the Flat to mean 'any two-dimensional system embedded in three dimensions that can, under the right conditions, exhibit topological order.' Different physics apply. In particular, it supports topological wormholes. Thus, the Flat can be nonlocal in the technical physics sense.

the-flat-title.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"><strong>The Flat</strong> — this book coins <em>the Flat</em> to mean "any two-dimensional system embedded in three dimensions that can, under the right conditions, exhibit topological order." Say that three times fast. Or say "Flat." <span class='hover-term' data-hover='A property where information is stored in global patterns, not in any single place. Extremely hard to destroy — you would have to disrupt the entire pattern at once, not just a piece of it.'>Different physics apply</span>. In particular, the Flat supports topological wormholes. The Flat can be nonlocal in the technical physics sense.</p><p style="margin:0;font-size:0.85em;line-height:1.4;color:#666;">The Flat inside your computer chip is a 2DEG (two-dimensional electron gas). The Flat in Earth's magnetosphere is plasma confined to thin current sheets. We call the ocean floor the Deep and the emptiness between galaxies the Void. This book calls those thin worlds <strong>the Flat</strong>. It's unclear what temperature range and conditions permits Flat behavior.</p><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="300" height="130" viewBox="0 0 300 130" style="display:block;margin:0.5em auto;"><title>Cross-section of a computer chip. Two grey blocks of 3D semiconductor sandwich a thin blue layer — the two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG). Blue dots are electrons confined to this flat layer. They can move left and right but not up or down. This is the Flat.</title><defs><linearGradient id="flat-glow" x1="0%" y1="0%" x2="100%" y2="0%"><stop offset="0%" stop-color="#2471a3" stop-opacity="0.05"/><stop offset="50%" stop-color="#2471a3" three-possibilities.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.4em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"><strong>A)</strong> Confabulation and lies — a pattern-matching mind built coherence from noise. <strong>B)</strong> Exaggerated kernel of truth — real people &amp; real programs inflated in the telling. <strong>C)</strong> Substantially true.</p><p style="margin:0;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;">The book calls these A, B, and C. The science of The Stack and The Flat is true, regardless. The only question is whether someone got there first.</p>
three-possibilities.text: A) Confabulation. B) Exaggerated kernel. C) Substantially true. The science is real regardless.

threshold.text: Below: nothing happens. Above: everything changes. A match needs a minimum temperature to ignite. A network needs a minimum complexity to self-organize. Phase transitions in physics.

topological-order.text: A phase of matter whose quantum state is held together by connections between distant points — real nonlocal channels built into the material. Those channels are what this book calls topological wormholes. Noise cannot easily erase information stored in them. (2016 Nobel Prize in Physics.) [Nayak et al., Rev. Mod. Phys. 80 (2008) 1083, doi:10.1103/RevModPhys.80.1083]

topological-protection.text: Information stored in global patterns is shielded from local disturbances — including heat. This is why topological quantum computers could work at room temperature, when conventional designs require near-absolute-zero cooling.

topological-wormhole.text: Fold the paper so two points touch — the signal doesn't cross the distance; the distance disappears. Different physics from spacetime wormholes (Einstein-Rosen bridges) — information only, inside a material, no faster-than-light travel. Real — 2016 Nobel Prize.

two-dimensional-electron-gas.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"><strong>Two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG)</strong> — a thin layer where electrons can only move in two directions, trapped on a sheet. Found inside every computer chip (at the oxide interface of a MOSFET) and in Earth's magnetic field (charged particles confined to thin plasma sheets). Different physics applies in two dimensions than in three.</p><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="300" height="130" viewBox="0 0 300 130" style="display:block;margin:0.5em auto;"><title>Cross-section: two grey blocks of 3D semiconductor sandwich a thin blue layer — the 2DEG. Blue dots are electrons confined to this flat layer. They can move left and right but not up or down.</title><defs><linearGradient id="deg-glow" x1="0%" y1="0%" x2="100%" y2="0%"><stop offset="0%" stop-color="#2471a3" stop-opacity="0.05"/><stop two-dimensional-electron-gas.text: A thin layer where electrons can only move in two directions — trapped on a sheet. Found inside every computer chip and in Earth's magnetic field.

universal-declaration-of-human-rights.text: A 1948 United Nations document listing 30 basic rights for all people. Written in the aftermath of World War II to say 'never again.' Not the law of any country — a set of principles that transcend borders.

wormholes.text: Not the sci-fi kind. Spacetime wormholes (Interstellar) fold spacetime itself — mass and all, across light-years. Topological wormholes are far more limited: they fold only a two-dimensional quantum material, never spacetime. Information crosses, not mass. No light-year shortcuts. No time travel. Inside the material only. Real physics — 2016 Nobel Prize — but restricted.

Inside the material only. Real physics — 2016 Nobel Prize — but restricted.

wormholes-title.html: <p style="margin:0 0 0.5em;font-size:0.95em;line-height:1.5;"><strong>Wormholes</strong> — real topological connections between distant points in a material. Not science fiction — condensed matter physics (2016 Nobel Prize). There's currently no evidence that anyone actually uses topological wormhole technology.</p><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="300" height="140" viewBox="0 0 300 140" style="display:block;margin:0.5em auto;"><title>Two surfaces (A and B) connected by a topological tunnel. Information at surface A appears at surface B without crossing the space between. The dashed lines show the connection threading through the material.</title><ellipse cx="80" cy="35" rx="65" ry="20" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="1.2" opacity="0.6"/><ellipse cx="80" cy="35" rx="18" ry="8" fill="#e8f0f8" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="220" cy="105" rx="65" ry="20" fill="none" stroke="#1a5276" stroke-width="1.2" opacity="0.6"/><ellipse cx="220" cy="105" rx="18" ry="8" fill="#e8f0f8" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="1.5"/><path d="M 62,35 C 55,60 195,80 202,105" fill="none" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/><path d="M 98,35 C 105,60 245,80 238,105" fill="none" stroke="#2471a3" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/><text x="80" y="12" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#888" font-family="serif" font-style="italic">surface A</text><text x="220" y="135" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#888" font-family="serif" font-style="italic">surface B</text><text x="150" y="75" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#2471a3" font-family="serif">topological</text><text x="150" y="87" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#2471a3" font-family="serif">connection</text></svg>
